content.problemStatement:
Given two strings sss,ttt, Alice calls a string ttt similar to string sss if all the characters(including duplicates) present in the string ttt are present in string sss.

Find the shortest substring of the string sss which is similar to ttt to make Alice happy.
If there is no such substring, print “IMPOSSIBLE” without the quotes.

String aaa is a substring of string bbb if it is possible to choose several consecutive letters in bbb in such a way that they form aaa.


Input Format

The first line will contain 2 integers ∣S∣,∣T∣|S|,|T|∣S∣,∣T∣
The second line contains the string SSS.
The third line contains the string TTT.


Output Format
Output the length of the shortest similar substring and the substring as well on different lines. If multiple answers are possible then output the earliest substring of the string sss. If there is no such substring, print “IMPOSSIBLE” without the quotes.
constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer Q, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with two integers |S| and |T| (the lengths of the strings, 1 ≤ |S|, |T| ≤ 10^5).
- A line with the string S.
- A line with the string T.
All strings consist of printable ASCII characters without spaces.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output either:
- Two lines: first the length of the shortest substring of S that is similar to T, second the substring itself; or
- A single line containing the word IMPOSSIBLE if no such substring exists.
Outputs for different test cases are printed consecutively.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Substrings {abcbcde,abcbcd} follow the similar substring condition out of which abcbcd is the shortest.

solution.approach: The problem is exactly the Minimum Window Substring problem. For each test case we count the required frequency of every character in T (need map). Then we slide a window [l, r) over S using two pointers. We expand r, updating a have map. When the window satisfies all required counts (i.e., for every character c, have[c] ≥ need[c]), we try to shrink l while the condition still holds, thereby obtaining the smallest window ending at r. Each time a valid window is found we compare its length with the best one seen so far; if it is shorter, or equal but starts earlier, we store it. The sliding window moves each pointer at most |S| steps, giving O(|S| + |T|) time per test case. The maps are arrays of size 128 (ASCII) for O(1) access, so the memory usage is O(1) besides the input strings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S| + |T|) per test case, overall O(total length of all S and T).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra (fixed size arrays of length 128) plus the input strings.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve_one():
    n, m = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    S = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    T = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    need = [0]*128
    have = [0]*128
    required = 0
    for ch in T:
        idx = ord(ch)
        if need[idx] == 0:
            required += 1
        need[idx] += 1
    formed = 0
    left = 0
    best_len = n + 1
    best_start = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(S):
        idx = ord(ch)
        have[idx] += 1
        if have[idx] == need[idx] and need[idx] > 0:
            formed += 1
        while formed == required and left <= right:
            cur_len = right - left + 1
            if cur_len < best_len:
                best_len = cur_len
                best_start = left
            lch = S[left]
            lidx = ord(lch)
            if have[lidx] == need[lidx] and need[lidx] > 0:
                formed -= 1
            have[lidx] -= 1
            left += 1
    if best_len == n + 1:
        return "IMPOSSIBLE"
    return f"{best_len}\n{S[best_start:best_start+best_len]}"

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    q = int(data[0])
    idx = 1
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(q):
        n, m = map(int, data[idx].split()); idx += 1
        S = data[idx].strip(); idx += 1
        T = data[idx].strip(); idx += 1
        # reuse solve_one logic but with given strings
        need = [0]*128
        have = [0]*128
        required = 0
        for ch in T:
            ci = ord(ch)
            if need[ci] == 0:
                required += 1
            need[ci] += 1
        formed = 0
        left = 0
        best_len = n + 1
        best_start = 0
        for right, ch in enumerate(S):
            ci = ord(ch)
            have[ci] += 1
            if have[ci] == need[ci] and need[ci] > 0:
                formed += 1
            while formed == required and left <= right:
                cur_len = right - left + 1
                if cur_len < best_len:
                    best_len = cur_len
                    best_start = left
                lch = S[left]
                li = ord(lch)
                if have[li] == need[li] and need[li] > 0:
                    formed -= 1
                have[li] -= 1
                left += 1
        if best_len == n + 1:
            out_lines.append("IMPOSSIBLE")
        else:
            out_lines.append(str(best_len))
            out_lines.append(S[best_start:best_start+best_len])
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
